why-version-control-matters.label: Why Version Control Matters
why-version-control-matters.summary: Meet Git, review the small terminal skills you need, and see how three projects build the same inspect-change-verify habit.

sketch1.title: Three Small Projects, One Learning Pattern

intro-memory.title: Before You Begin
intro-memory.bodyMarkdown:
Welcome to **Git Foundations**. Git is a version control system: it records meaningful snapshots of a project so you can see what changed, understand why it changed, and return to an earlier state when needed.

In this course, you will work step by step in the terminal to initialize a local repository, inspect its state, stage selected changes, create clear commits, read history, manage tracked files, ignore generated output, and recover from common mistakes.

**Linux Terminal Fundamentals** is recommended, but it is not required. You only need basic terminal navigation: recognize files and folders, use `pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `mkdir`, and `touch`, and open and save a plain-text file. You do **not** need programming, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GitHub, or previous Git experience. Whenever a file must change, the lesson gives you the exact path and exact text.

worked-example.title: Three Small Projects, One Learning Pattern
worked-example.bodyMarkdown:
The guided lessons grow `community-project/` across Modules 1–3. After each module, you apply the same ideas in `volunteer-project/` with less guidance. The final capstone begins fresh in `neighborhood-guide/`, where you build a complete local history independently.

Each workspace stays small and text-based so the lesson remains focused on Git. Across all three projects, the pattern is the same: **inspect the current state → make one deliberate change → verify the result**.
